title: Session Management
html:
Session Management

  • Last UpdatedSept 14, 2024

    • 10.4

Session Management

In a Uniface web application, an HTTP session is a lasting connection between a web user (the browser) and the Uniface Web Application Server (WASV). Uniface automatically generates a unique ID for each session, which it uses to bind state information held on the server with the current session.

The session ID is a vulnerable point for web security breaches, so it is usually necessary to manage sessions to enforce authorization checks and prevent common attacks. One way of doing this is to regenerate sessions with new session IDs at critical points in the application process, such as after login or logout or before committing data. In addition to user state information required by the application, you can also control session state, and context information that needs to be available for the duration of the HTTP request-response cycle.

Uniface provides the following functionality for implementing session management:

  • $webinfo—this ProcScript command has specialized parameters for accessing the session ID, session management commands, session attributes, and context data for the Uniface Server.
  • Session Management API—provides an interface to the Web Request Dispatcher (WRD), enabling you to instruct it to start a session, close a session, or start a new session with old session's context (session state and attributes).

Session ID

Uniface generates a unique session ID for each session. This is used to tie state held on the server with the session in progress.

You can access the session ID using the SESSION property of the $webinfo("WEBSERVERCONTEXT") channel.

However, the session ID is vulnerable to attacks in which the session ID is stolen and then used to mimic a user and gain further access to the application. Also, because the session ID is unique to the browser, it can be reused by the next user on the same browser. For web security guidelines for session management, see Session Management for Web Applications.

One way to defend against such attacks is to regenerate the session ID at critical points in the session, such as after login. Uniface provides a session management API, which provides an interface to the Web Request Dispatcher (WRD) that enables you to do this.

The API also enables you to specify session attributes for each session, which you can use to provide your own state, or other information about the session.

Session Context

Session attributes are user-defined data that enable you to maintain additional information about the session. You have complete control over the names and values of session attributes. Using the session management API, you can call back to a component, instruct the WRD to set or delete session attributes, or invoke an operation on the calling component that does something with the session context or security checks.

You can call the session API commands using the $webinfo("SESSIONCOMMANDS") channel.

For an example of how to use the API to change the session ID, see Session Fixation.

Request Context

Request context attributes are user-defined data that can be shared between an HTTP request for a DSP and any other DSP instances that are activated as a result of callback operations.

The Uniface Server creates the request state when it first receives the HTTP request, and destroys it when it returns the response to the browser.

To get or set the request state information you want to share between the first HTTP request and possible callback requests, use the $webinfo("REQUESTCONTEXT") function.

To activate a callback operation, use the WrdActivate property of $webinfo("SESSIONCOMMANDS").

Related Links
  • APIs: Session Management

    The session management API provides an interface to the Web Request Dispatcher (WRD) that enables you to change and control the HTTP session. You can invalidate the current session, or create a new session with (or without) the same state information and session attributes.

  • $webinfo

    Return or set information about the current HTTP request or response.

  • $webinfo ("SessionCommands")

    Use $webinfo("SessionCommands" to invoke session management API calls.

  • $webinfo ("WebServerContext")

    The WebServerContext topic contains an associative list of properties that provide state information about the Uniface Server and the context in which it is running.

  • $webinfo ("RequestContext")

    Get or set request context attributes.
